title: Italian vein marbling
subtitle: Producing the branching vein pattern of Italian marble simulation
excerpt: Italian vein marbling imitates the geological appearance of veined stone: branching dark lines on a pale ground, created by dragging a stylus through pooled colours on a carrageenan size. Unlike combed patterns, vein marbling uses no comb, the pattern emerges from the resistance between the background colour and the veining colour.
sourceNotes: Anne Chambers, A Practical Guide to Marbling Paper (1986, Thames and Hudson), chapter on stone and vein patterns. The Italian vein technique described here follows Chambers's instructions for 'Antique Marble', the branch-and-vein variant from the Florentine tradition.

content.text: is likely the oldest in the Western marbling tradition and the most immediately convincing as a geological simulation. It requires no comb, just colours dropped in a layered background, then a
drawn through to create branching lines.

For a Bianco Carrara simulation: background in white with pale grey and cream, vein lines in charcoal. For Rosso Levanto: deep burgundy background, black and cream veins. For Verde Antico: mottled green background, white veins. The background colours are laid as large drops that spread to fill the tray; the veining colour is added last in a separate thin trail.

Drop large spots of the background colours across the size, allowing them to spread and crowd each other. Two or three background colours produce more geological complexity than one. Do not stir, the natural spreading and resistance between colours creates the mottled background. Fill the tray to within 10 mm of the edges before adding veins.

Load the eyedropper with the vein colour (more ox-gall added than the background colours: 3-4 drops per 30 ml, so it spreads very thinly). Trail the vein colour across the tray in a diagonal from corner to corner, letting it fall in a narrow stream. Immediately pass the stylus along the vein trail, pulling at angles to create side branches: enter the main vein from the left at 30° intervals, pulling 2-3 cm into the background colour.

Working quickly, do not re-arrange the pattern after the first stylus pass, lower the alum-prepared sheet onto the size face-down. The pattern transfers within 30 seconds. Lift from one corner, rinse under cool running water, and hang to dry. Each vein pattern is unique; the slight irregularities are what makes veined marble convincing.
